content.text: A generative model learns the joint distribution p(x, y) or, in unsupervised settings, the marginal distribution p(x) over inputs. Rather than drawing a decision boundary between classes, it builds a statistical model of how data is generated — what values of x are plausible, what combinations of features tend to co-occur, and how likely any given input is. This generative view unlocks capabilities that discriminative models cannot provide: sampling new data, reasoning under missing observations, and detecting anomalies through low likelihood.
The Joint vs Conditional Distribution
A generative model explicitly represents p(x, y) = p(x | y) · p(y). Bayes' theorem then recovers the conditional needed for classification:

attrs.latex: p(y \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid y)\, p(y)}{p(x)} = \frac{p(x \mid y)\, p(y)}{\sum_{y'} p(x \mid y')\, p(y')}

content.text: In unsupervised settings there are no labels and the model learns p(x) directly. Regardless of which form is used, the defining feature is that p(x) — the probability of the input itself — is part of the model. This is what enables generation: sampling x ~ p(x) or x ~ p(x | y = cat).
What Generative Models Learn
Training a generative model on a dataset {x_i} means maximising the marginal log-likelihood:

This objective requires assigning high probability to training data and, implicitly, lower probability to everything else. The model must account for all the variation in x: for images, this means modelling textures, shapes, lighting, and composition simultaneously. This is a much harder problem than learning a decision boundary, which explains why generative models typically require more data and compute than discriminative ones to reach comparable classification accuracy.
Taxonomy of Generative Models
Generative models differ in how they represent and compute p(x). Three fundamental families:
Family
How p(x) is defined
Tractable likelihood?
Examples
Explicit likelihood
Direct formula for p(x; θ)
Yes — maximise directly
Autoregressive (GPT), normalising flows
Approximate likelihood
Variational lower bound (ELBO)
No — maximise ELBO instead
VAE, diffusion models
Implicit likelihood
Samples only, no formula
No — adversarial/score objectives
GAN, energy-based models
Autoregressive Models: Factoring the Joint
The most principled way to define p(x) for sequential data is the chain rule factorisation:

This is exactly what GPT-style language models learn. Each factor p(x_t | x_{<t}) is modelled by a neural network (Transformer), and training maximises the sum of log-factors over all positions and all sequences. The likelihood is tractable because the product decomposes into tractable per-token conditionals. Generation samples x_t ~ p(x_t | x_{<t}) autoregressively from left to right.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch import Tensor


class SimpleAutoregressiveGenerativeModel(nn.Module):
    """
    Autoregressive generative model: factorises p(x) = prod_t p(x_t | x_{<t}).
    Learns the joint distribution p(x) via conditional next-token prediction.
    Unlike a discriminative classifier, the training signal is the data
    distribution itself — no labels needed.
    """
    def __init__(self, vocab_size: int, d_model: int = 256, n_layers: int = 4):
        super().__init__()
        self.embed = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model)
        self.pos_embed = nn.Embedding(512, d_model)
        encoder_layer = nn.TransformerEncoderLayer(
            d_model, nhead=4, batch_first=True
        )
        self.transformer = nn.TransformerEncoder(encoder_layer, n_layers)
        self.lm_head = nn.Linear(d_model, vocab_size)

    def forward(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """Returns logits for p(x_t | x_{<t}) at every position."""
        T = x.size(1)
        positions = torch.arange(T, device=x.device)
        h = self.embed(x) + self.pos_embed(positions)
        # causal mask: each position attends only to past tokens
        mask = nn.Transformer.generate_square_subsequent_mask(T, device=x.device)
        h = self.transformer(h, mask=mask, is_causal=True)
        return self.lm_head(h)   # (B, T, vocab_size)

    def nll(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """
        Negative log-likelihood: -log p(x) = sum_t -log p(x_t | x_{<t}).
        This IS the likelihood of the data under the model — not a label loss.
        """
        logits = self.forward(x[:, :-1])   # (B, T-1, V) — predict next from context
        targets = x[:, 1:]                  # (B, T-1) — next tokens as targets
        return nn.functional.cross_entropy(
            logits.reshape(-1, logits.size(-1)), targets.reshape(-1)
        )

    @torch.no_grad()
    def sample(self, prompt: Tensor, max_new_tokens: int = 50) -> Tensor:
        """
        Ancestral sampling: x_t ~ p(x_t | x_{<t}) for t = T+1, ...
        This is generation — the core capability that discriminative models lack.
        """
        x = prompt.clone()
        for _ in range(max_new_tokens):
            logits = self.forward(x)[:, -1, :]   # (B, V) — last position
            next_token = torch.multinomial(torch.softmax(logits, dim=-1), 1)
            x = torch.cat([x, next_token], dim=1)
        return x
Latent Variable Models
Many generative models introduce a latent variable z that represents a compact, abstract description of x. The joint is p(x, z) = p(x | z) · p(z), and the marginal is obtained by integrating out z:

This integral is generally intractable — evaluating p(x) requires integrating over all possible z values. VAEs handle this via a variational lower bound (ELBO); diffusion models handle it by defining the latent z as a sequence of noised versions of x. Generation is done by sampling z ~ p(z) and then decoding x ~ p(x | z).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch import Tensor


class GaussianMixtureGenerative(nn.Module):
    """
    Simplest latent variable generative model: Gaussian Mixture Model.
    p(x) = sum_k pi_k * N(x; mu_k, Sigma_k)
    where z = k (the mixture component) is the discrete latent variable.

    p(x) = sum_k p(z=k) * p(x | z=k)

    Generation: sample z ~ Categorical(pi), then x ~ N(mu_z, Sigma_z).
    Classification via Bayes' rule: p(z | x) = p(x | z) p(z) / p(x).
    """
    def __init__(self, n_components: int = 5, dim: int = 2):
        super().__init__()
        self.n_components = n_components
        self.log_pi = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(n_components))    # log mixing weights
        self.means = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(n_components, dim))
        self.log_stds = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(n_components, dim))

    def log_prob(self, x: Tensor) -> Tensor:
        """Evaluate log p(x) = log sum_k pi_k * N(x; mu_k, sigma_k)."""
        log_pi = self.log_pi - torch.logsumexp(self.log_pi, dim=0)  # normalise
        stds = self.log_stds.exp().clamp(min=1e-4)
        # (B, K) log p(x | z=k) for each component
        log_px_given_z = torch.distributions.Normal(
            self.means, stds
        ).log_prob(x.unsqueeze(1)).sum(-1)  # (B, K)
        # log p(x) = logsumexp_k [log pi_k + log p(x | z=k)]
        return torch.logsumexp(log_pi.unsqueeze(0) + log_px_given_z, dim=1)  # (B,)

    def sample(self, n: int, device=None) -> Tensor:
        """Sample x ~ p(x) by ancestral sampling: z ~ pi, x ~ p(x|z)."""
        log_pi = self.log_pi - torch.logsumexp(self.log_pi, dim=0)
        z = torch.multinomial(log_pi.exp(), n, replacement=True)   # (n,)
        means = self.means[z]                                        # (n, d)
        stds = self.log_stds.exp()[z]                               # (n, d)
        return torch.randn_like(means) * stds + means               # (n, d)
What Generative Models Enable
Sample generation: draw new x ~ p(x) or x ~ p(x | y = c) for a given class c. This is the defining capability — text generation, image synthesis, audio synthesis.
Density estimation: evaluate p(x) for any input, enabling anomaly detection (low p(x) = unusual), compression (high p(x) = compressible), and likelihood-based model comparison.
Missing data handling: because p(x) is modelled, computing p(x_observed) by marginalising over missing dimensions is principled.
Semi-supervised learning: unlabelled data can be used to improve p(x), which in turn helps p(y | x) through Bayes' theorem.
Inpainting and completion: p(x_missing | x_observed) = p(x) / p(x_observed) — a conditional derived from the generative model.
The Harder Objective: Why Generative Models Struggle at Classification

To classify via a generative model, compute p(y | x) ∝ p(x | y) · p(y) for each class y and pick the highest. This requires a high-quality model of p(x | y) — essentially a separate model of what images of cats look like vs what images of dogs look like. A single mistake in modelling the data distribution (e.g., overestimating the likelihood of blurry images for one class) directly corrupts the classifier. In contrast, a discriminative model only needs to capture the features that differ between classes, ignoring everything else.

Ng & Jordan (2002) showed formally that naive Bayes (a generative classifier) reaches its asymptotic error faster with fewer training examples, but logistic regression (discriminative) achieves lower asymptotic error with enough data. Generative models win in the low-data regime; discriminative models win when data is abundant.
Modern Generative Models Are Foundation Models

Large-scale generative pre-training has become the dominant paradigm in AI. GPT models are trained on the objective of maximising log p(x) over internet-scale text — a purely generative objective. The resulting model learns rich representations that can be steered for discriminative tasks through prompting, fine-tuning, or RLHF. This reverses the traditional classification picture: rather than choosing discriminative over generative for efficiency, practitioners train a single large generative model and adapt it to any downstream task.

Mental model: a generative model is a world simulator — it has internalised enough about the data distribution to conjure new examples that are statistically indistinguishable from real ones. A discriminative model is a classifier — it knows how to separate categories but has no internal model of what each category looks like. The generative model can answer 'show me a cat'; the discriminative model can only answer 'is this a cat?' Modern large language models are generative simulators of text whose simulation is rich enough that they can answer both kinds of question.
Key Takeaways
Generative models represent p(x) or p(x, y), enabling sampling, density estimation, and principled handling of missing data — capabilities absent from discriminative models.
Training maximises marginal log-likelihood log p(x), which is a strictly harder objective than the conditional log-likelihood log p(y | x) used by discriminative models.
Three families: explicit likelihood (autoregressive, flows), approximate likelihood (VAE, diffusion), and implicit likelihood (GAN, EBM), each with different sampling and training trade-offs.
Generative models win in the low-data regime and for semi-supervised learning; discriminative models win when labelled data is abundant and the task is classification.

Large-scale autoregressive language models (GPT family) are generative models that have become general-purpose foundation models — their generative objective produces representations strong enough for discriminative tasks via fine-tuning or prompting.
